annotations.label: Definite integral

anchor.snippet: A definite integral computes the signed area of the region in the plane that is bounded by the graph of a given function between two points in the real line

annotations.note: `intervalIntegral f a b μ` is the signed integral of `f` from `a` to `b`.

annotations.label: Indefinite integral / antiderivative

anchor.snippet: Integrals also refer to the concept of an antiderivative , a function whose derivative is the given function

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named `antiderivative` definition; antiderivatives are stated via `HasDerivAt F' = f` hypotheses.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of calculus (lead statement)

anchor.snippet: The fundamental theorem of calculus relates definite integration to differentiation and provides a method to compute the definite integral of a function when its antiderivative is known

annotations.note: FTC-2 is `∫ x in a..b, f' x = F b - F a` when `HasDerivAt F (f' x) x`.

annotations.label: Line integral

anchor.snippet: a line integral is defined for functions of two or more variables, and the interval of integration is replaced by a curve connecting two points in space

annotations.note: No general line integral over a curve is defined in Mathlib (no `curveIntegral` decl exists); only `intervalIntegral` over an interval and `circleIntegral` for circles.

annotations.label: Surface integral

anchor.snippet: In a surface integral , the curve is replaced by a piece of a surface in three-dimensional space

annotations.note: No general surface integral over a parametric 2-surface appears in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: Cavalieri computing the integrals of x n up to degree n = 9 in Cavalieri's quadrature formula

annotations.label: Integral notation

anchor.snippet: In general, the integral of a real-valued function f ( x ) with respect to a real variable x on an interval [ a , b ] is written as

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the notation `∫ x in a..b, f x` for `intervalIntegral`.

annotations.label: Integrable function

anchor.snippet: A function is said to be integrable if its integral over its domain is finite

annotations.note: `Integrable f μ` means `f` is a.e. strongly measurable with finite `lintegral` of `‖f‖`.

annotations.label: Indefinite integral

anchor.snippet: the integral is called an indefinite integral, which represents a class of functions (the antiderivative ) whose derivative is the integrand

annotations.note: There is no named indefinite-integral/antiderivative constant in Mathlib; the relation is expressed pointwise via `HasDerivAt`.

annotations.label: Riemann approximation of area under √x

anchor.snippet: to find the area of the region bounded by the graph of the function f ( x ) =

annotations.note: This is an illustrative numerical example, not a formal statement.

annotations.label: Tagged partition

anchor.snippet: A tagged partition of a closed interval [ a , b ] on the real line is a finite sequence

annotations.note: `TaggedPrepartition I` formalizes tagged partitions of boxes (generalizing intervals).

annotations.label: Riemann sum

anchor.snippet: A Riemann sum of a function f with respect to such a tagged partition is defined as

annotations.note: `integralSum f vol π` is the Riemann sum of `f` over a tagged prepartition `π`.

annotations.label: Mesh of a partition

anchor.snippet: The mesh of such a tagged partition is the width of the largest sub-interval formed by the partition

annotations.note: Mathlib's BoxIntegral uses `distortion` and `isSubordinate` rather than a scalar mesh.

annotations.label: Riemann integral

anchor.snippet: The Riemann integral of a function f over the interval [ a , b ] is equal to S if:

annotations.note: `BoxIntegral.integral` with the Riemann filter generalizes the Riemann integral to boxes.

annotations.label: Darboux sums

anchor.snippet: When the chosen tags are the maximum (respectively, minimum) value of the function in each interval, the Riemann sum becomes an upper (respectively, lower) Darboux sum

annotations.note: No upper/lower Darboux sums are defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lebesgue integral of non-negative function

anchor.snippet: the integral of a non-negative function f : R → R should be the sum over t of the areas between a thin horizontal strip

annotations.note: `lintegral μ f` is the lower Lebesgue integral of an `ℝ≥0∞`-valued function.

annotations.label: Lebesgue-integrable function

anchor.snippet: A general measurable function f is Lebesgue-integrable if the sum of the absolute values of the areas of the regions between the graph of f and the x -axis is finite

annotations.note: `Integrable f μ` requires `∫⁻ ‖f x‖ ∂μ < ∞`.

annotations.label: Darboux integral

anchor.snippet: The Darboux integral , which is defined by Darboux sums (restricted Riemann sums) yet is equivalent to the Riemann integral

annotations.note: No Darboux integral is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Riemann–Stieltjes integral

anchor.snippet: The Riemann–Stieltjes integral , an extension of the Riemann integral which integrates with respect to a function as opposed to a variable

annotations.note: Mathlib has Stieltjes measures (used for Lebesgue–Stieltjes integration) but no explicit Riemann–Stieltjes construction.

annotations.label: Lebesgue–Stieltjes integral

anchor.snippet: The Lebesgue–Stieltjes integral , further developed by Johann Radon , which generalizes both the Riemann–Stieltjes and Lebesgue integrals

annotations.note: Lebesgue–Stieltjes integrals are realized by integrating against `StieltjesFunction.measure`, but no dedicated definition exists.

annotations.label: Daniell integral

anchor.snippet: The Daniell integral , which subsumes the Lebesgue integral and Lebesgue–Stieltjes integral without depending on measures

annotations.note: No Daniell integral construction exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Haar integral

anchor.snippet: The Haar integral , used for integration on locally compact topological groups, introduced by Alfréd Haar in 1933

annotations.note: Integration with respect to `haarMeasure` realises the Haar integral on a locally compact group.

annotations.label: Henstock–Kurzweil integral

anchor.snippet: The Henstock–Kurzweil integral , variously defined by Arnaud Denjoy , Oskar Perron

annotations.note: BoxIntegral with the Henstock filter is the gauge / Henstock–Kurzweil integral.

annotations.label: Khinchin integral

anchor.snippet: The Khinchin integral , named after Aleksandr Khinchin

annotations.note: No Khinchin integral appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Itô and Stratonovich integrals

anchor.snippet: The Itô integral and Stratonovich integral , which define integration with respect to semimartingales such as Brownian motion

annotations.note: No stochastic Itô or Stratonovich integral exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Young integral

anchor.snippet: The Young integral , which is a kind of Riemann–Stieltjes integral with respect to certain functions of unbounded variation

annotations.note: No Young integral exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rough path integral

anchor.snippet: The rough path integral, which is defined for functions equipped with some additional

annotations.note: No rough path theory exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Choquet integral

anchor.snippet: The Choquet integral , a subadditive or superadditive integral created by Gustave Choquet in 1953

annotations.note: No Choquet integral appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bochner integral

anchor.snippet: The Bochner integral , a generalization of the Lebesgue integral to functions that take values in a Banach space

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.integral μ f` is the Bochner integral of a Banach-space-valued function.

annotations.label: Linearity of the Riemann integral

anchor.snippet: the collection of integrable functions is closed under taking linear combinations , and the integral of a linear combination is the linear combination of the integrals

annotations.note: Additivity and scalar multiplication of the interval integral are proven (`integral_add`, `integral_smul`).

annotations.label: Linearity of the Lebesgue integral

anchor.snippet: the set of real -valued Lebesgue-integrable functions on a given measure space E with measure μ is closed under taking linear combinations and hence form a vector space

annotations.note: `integral_add` plus `integral_smul` give the linearity of the (Bochner/Lebesgue) integral on `L¹`.

annotations.label: Abstract integration map

anchor.snippet: Then one may define an abstract integration map assigning to each function f an element of V or the symbol ∞

annotations.note: Mathlib's `MeasureTheory.integral` is exactly the abstract integration map into a Banach space.

annotations.label: Upper and lower bounds

anchor.snippet: An integrable function f on [ a , b ] , is necessarily bounded on that interval

annotations.note: Mathlib's measure-theoretic integrability does not imply boundedness, so this Riemann-specific fact is not stated.

annotations.label: Inequalities between functions

anchor.snippet: If f ( x ) ≤ g ( x ) for each x in [ a , b ] then each of the upper and lower sums of f is bounded above by the upper and lower sums, respectively, of g

annotations.note: Monotonicity of the interval integral; the global form is `MeasureTheory.integral_mono`.

annotations.label: Subinterval inequality

anchor.snippet: If [ c , d ] is a subinterval of [ a , b ] and f ( x ) is non-negative for all x , then

annotations.note: Monotonicity of `setIntegral` under set inclusion for a.e. non-negative integrands.

annotations.label: Absolute value inequality

anchor.snippet: If f is Riemann-integrable on [ a , b ] then the same is true for | f |

annotations.note: `|∫ x in a..b, f x| ≤ ∫ x in a..b, |f x|`.

annotations.label: Cauchy–Schwarz inequality

anchor.snippet: This inequality, known as the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality , plays a prominent role in Hilbert space theory

annotations.note: Cauchy–Schwarz for integrals is the `p = q = 2` case of Hölder; no dedicated `integral`-form lemma.

annotations.label: Hölder's inequality

anchor.snippet: Then the functions | f | p and | g | q are also integrable and the following Hölder's inequality holds

annotations.note: Hölder's inequality for `lintegral` with conjugate exponents `p, q`.

annotations.label: Minkowski inequality

anchor.snippet: Then | f | p , | g | p and | f + g | p are also Riemann-integrable and the following Minkowski inequality holds

annotations.note: Minkowski's inequality for the `ℒp` seminorm of `f + g`.

annotations.label: Reversed limits convention

anchor.snippet: Integrals can also be defined if a > b :

annotations.note: `∫ x in a..b, f x = -∫ x in b..a, f x` is built into `intervalIntegral`.

annotations.label: Integral over a degenerate interval is zero

anchor.snippet: the second says that an integral taken over a degenerate interval, or a point , should be zero

annotations.note: `intervalIntegral.integral_same : ∫ x in a..a, f x ∂μ = 0`.

annotations.label: Additivity over subintervals

anchor.snippet: the integrability of f on an interval [ a , b ] implies that f is integrable on any subinterval [ c , d ] , but in particular integrals have the property that if c is any element of [ a , b ]

annotations.note: `∫ a..b + ∫ b..c = ∫ a..c` under interval integrability.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of calculus

anchor.snippet: The fundamental theorem of calculus is the statement that differentiation and integration are inverse operations

annotations.note: FTC (Newton–Leibniz form) is proved in `FundThmCalculus`.

annotations.label: First fundamental theorem of calculus

anchor.snippet: Let f be a continuous real-valued function defined on a closed interval [ a , b ]

annotations.note: Derivative of `x ↦ ∫ t in a..x, f t` equals `f x` at points of continuity.

annotations.label: Second fundamental theorem of calculus

anchor.snippet: Let f be a real-valued function defined on a closed interval [ a , b ] that admits an antiderivative F

annotations.note: If `HasDerivAt F (f x) x` on `[a,b]` and `f` is integrable, then `∫ a..b, f = F b - F a`.

annotations.label: Improper integral (unbounded interval)

anchor.snippet: If the interval is unbounded, for instance at its upper end, then the improper integral is the limit as that endpoint goes to infinity

annotations.note: `AECover` machinery identifies the limit of `∫ in Ioc a n, f` with the integral over the unbounded set.

annotations.label: Improper integral (half-open interval)

anchor.snippet: If the integrand is only defined or finite on a half-open interval, for instance ( a , b ] , then again a limit may provide a finite result

annotations.note: The same `AECover` framework handles improper integrals over half-open intervals.

annotations.label: Double integral

anchor.snippet: the double integral of a positive function of two variables represents the volume of the region between the surface defined by the function and the plane that contains its domain

annotations.note: Integration over a product measure realises double integrals; iterated form proved as `integral_prod`.

annotations.label: Fubini's theorem

anchor.snippet: Fubini's theorem states that this integral can be expressed as an equivalent iterated integral

annotations.note: `integral_prod` expresses `∫ z, f z ∂(μ.prod ν)` as the iterated integral.

annotations.label: Line integral

anchor.snippet: A line integral (sometimes called a path integral ) is an integral where the function to be integrated is evaluated along a curve

annotations.note: No general line integral over a curve is defined in Mathlib; only `intervalIntegral` (over an interval) and `circleIntegral` (over a circle) exist.

annotations.label: Contour integral (closed curve)

anchor.snippet: In the case of a closed curve it is also called a contour integral

annotations.note: Only the circle-parameterised closed contour `circleIntegral` is defined; no general closed-curve contour integral.

annotations.label: Work as a line integral

anchor.snippet: For an object moving along a path C in a vector field F such as an electric field or gravitational field , the total work done by the field on the object is obtained by summing up the differential work

annotations.note: This is a physics illustration without a dedicated Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Surface integral

anchor.snippet: A surface integral generalizes double integrals to integration over a surface

annotations.note: No surface-integral definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Flux as a surface integral

anchor.snippet: For an example of applications of surface integrals, consider a vector field v on a surface S

annotations.note: No notion of vector-field flux through a surface is formalized.

annotations.label: Contour integral

anchor.snippet: In complex analysis , the integrand is a complex-valued function of a complex variable z instead of a real function of a real variable x

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes `circleIntegral`; a generic complex contour integral is not separately defined.

annotations.label: Differential one-form

anchor.snippet: a one-form is a weighted sum of the differentials of the coordinates

annotations.note: Differential 1-forms are represented as fields valued in `E [⋀^Fin 1]→L[𝕜] F`; no dedicated `OneForm` abbreviation.

annotations.label: Differential two-form

anchor.snippet: A differential two-form is a sum of the form

annotations.note: 2-forms are encoded as fields valued in `E [⋀^Fin 2]→L[𝕜] F`; no dedicated `TwoForm` definition.

annotations.label: Stokes' theorem (generalization)

anchor.snippet: Stokes' theorem simultaneously generalizes the three theorems of vector calculus: the divergence theorem , Green's theorem , and the Kelvin-Stokes theorem

annotations.note: The divergence theorem on rectangles is proved; the general Stokes theorem on manifolds is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Functional integral

anchor.snippet: An integration that is performed not over a variable (or, in physics, over a space or time dimension), but over a space of functions , is referred to as a functional integral

annotations.note: No path/functional integral formalism appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Probability density integral equals 1

anchor.snippet: the integral under an entire probability density function must equal 1, which provides a test of whether a function with no negative values could be a density function

annotations.note: `HasPDF` characterises densities, but `∫ pdf = 1` is not stated as a single named lemma.

annotations.label: Volume by disc integration

anchor.snippet: The volume of a three-dimensional object such as a disc or washer can be computed by disc integration using the equation for the volume of a cylinder

annotations.note: No disc-integration formula for volumes of revolution exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Displacement from velocity

anchor.snippet: in rectilinear motion , the displacement of an object over the time interval

annotations.note: A physics example, not stated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Work done by a force

anchor.snippet: The work done by a force

annotations.note: A physics example, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Antiderivative evaluation formula

anchor.snippet: find an antiderivative of f ; that is, a function F such that F ′ = f on the interval

annotations.note: Evaluating via an antiderivative is FTC-2.

annotations.label: Trapezoidal rule

anchor.snippet: the trapezoidal rule , replaces the rectangles used in a Riemann sum with trapezoids

annotations.note: No dedicated trapezoidal-rule definition exists in Mathlib (no `trapezoidal_integral` decl).

annotations.label: Simpson's rule

anchor.snippet: Simpson's rule approximates the integrand by a piecewise quadratic function

annotations.note: Simpson's rule is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Newton–Cotes formulas

anchor.snippet: Riemann sums, the trapezoidal rule, and Simpson's rule are examples of a family of quadrature rules called the Newton–Cotes formulas

annotations.note: Newton–Cotes quadrature is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Romberg's method

anchor.snippet: Romberg's method halves the step widths incrementally, giving trapezoid approximations

annotations.note: Romberg integration is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gaussian quadrature

anchor.snippet: Gaussian quadrature evaluates the function at the roots of a set of orthogonal polynomials

annotations.note: Gaussian quadrature is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exactness of n-point Gaussian quadrature

anchor.snippet: An n -point Gaussian method is exact for polynomials of degree up to 2 n − 1

annotations.note: No statement about Gaussian quadrature exactness exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integration by differentiation (Kempf–Jackson–Morales)

anchor.snippet: Kempf, Jackson and Morales demonstrated mathematical relations that allow an integral to be calculated by means of differentiation

annotations.note: This recent technique is not formalized in Mathlib.
